claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 19 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 19 and in approved education
  - Another claimant who lives with the child has priority
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child in hospital for 11 weeks (within 12-week limit)
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child works 24+ hours/week but is in approved education
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Another claimant who lives with the child has priority
circumstances: Child is 19 and in approved education
Another claimant who lives with the child has priority
Child is not in local authority care
Child in hospital for 11 weeks (within 12-week limit)
Child works 24+ hours/week but is in approved education
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit in the UK.

To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you some questions about your circumstances. Let me start with the basics:

**Do you have any children that you're responsible for?** If yes, could you please tell me:
- How many children you have
- Their names (or just first names)
- Their ages

This will help me understand your situation and guide you through the eligibility assessment.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I do have a child. I have one child - Alex, who is 19 years old and lives with me at home.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! I can see that you have one child, Alex, who is 19 years old and lives with you.

Since Alex is 19, I need to ask some additional questions to determine eligibility, as Child Benefit typically covers children under 16, or under 20 if they're in approved education or training.

**Is Alex currently in approved education or training?** For example:
- Full-time non-advanced education (such as studying for A-levels, Scottish Highers, or similar qualifications)
- Approved training courses (like certain vocational training programs)

Please note that university or other advanced education courses do not count as approved education for Child Benefit purposes.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Alex is in approved education - he's studying for his A-levels at the local sixth form college.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! That's helpful to know.

Since Alex is 19 and studying for A-levels, which is approved education, there are a few more questions I need to ask:

**When did Alex turn 19?** (Please provide the month and year, or how many months ago)

This is important because Child Benefit can continue until the 31st of August following a young person's 19th birthday, as long as they remain in approved education.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: He turned 19 about 5 months ago, so that would have been back in October.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. So Alex turned 19 in October, which means he's currently 19 and studying for his A-levels.

I need to clarify one more important detail:

**When is Alex expected to finish his A-level course?** Specifically:
- Is he finishing this academic year (in summer, around June/July)?
- Or is he continuing into another academic year beyond this August?

This matters because Child Benefit continues for young people in approved education only until the 31st of August following their 19th birthday. Since Alex turned 19 in October (a few months ago), the cutoff date for him would be 31st August of this year.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: He's continuing beyond this August - he won't finish his A-levels until next summer.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing all that information. Let me make sure I understand your situation correctly:

- Alex is 19 years old (turned 19 in October)
- He's studying for A-levels at sixth form college (approved education)
- He lives with you
- He won't finish his A-levels until next summer (beyond August of this year)

Before I give you my final assessment, I need to ask one more question:

**Are you the person responsible for Alex?** In other words, do you have parental responsibility for him and are you the main person caring for him?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I am responsible for Alex. I'm his parent and he lives with me at home.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for confirming all of that information. I now have enough details to assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex.

Let me provide you with my assessment:

**Unfortunately, you would not be eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.**

Here's why: While Alex is in approved education (A-levels), which normally allows Child Benefit to continue beyond age 16, there is an age limit. Child Benefit only continues for young people in approved education until **31st August following their 19th birthday**.

Since Alex turned 19 in October (which was about 5 months ago), the 31st August deadline has already passed - it would have been the August *before* he turned 19. This means Child Benefit eligibility ended on 31st August before his 19th birthday in October.

Even though Alex is still studying for his A-levels and living with you, he has passed the maximum age limit for Child Benefit eligibility.

I'm sorry this isn't the outcome you were hoping for, but I hope this explanation helps clarify the situation.

Is there anything else you'd like to know about Child Benefit?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
Oh, I see. That's disappointing, but thank you for explaining it clearly. Just to make sure I understand - so even though he's still in college doing his A-levels, because he's already past that August deadline after turning 19, I can't claim for him anymore?

Also, I should mention - he has been in hospital for a couple of months recently. Does that make any difference to the eligibility?

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
You're absolutely right in your understanding - even though Alex is still in college doing his A-levels, because he's past the 31st August following his 19th birthday, the age limit has been exceeded.

Regarding Alex being in hospital for a couple of months - that's an important detail, but unfortunately **it doesn't change the eligibility in this case**. 

Here's why: The rules do allow Child Benefit to continue for children and young people who are in hospital (or in certain types of care), but this is still subject to the same age limits. Since Alex is 19 and has already passed the 31st August deadline after his 19th birthday, the hospital stay doesn't extend eligibility beyond that age limit.

The hospital provision is mainly to ensure that parents don't lose Child Benefit just because their child is temporarily in hospital, but it doesn't override the fundamental age requirements.

So to summarize your situation:
- Alex turned 19 in October
- The 31st August before that was his cutoff date
- Even with approved education (A-levels) and the hospital stay, eligibility cannot continue past that deadline

I know this is disappointing news. Is there anything else I can help clarify about Child Benefit?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
No, I think I understand now. It's a shame, but at least I know where I stand. Thank you for taking the time to explain everything to me.

Actually, wait - I should probably mention one other thing. Alex's other parent also lives with us, and they've been claiming the Child Benefit for Alex. Does that change anything, or does the same age rule apply to them as well?

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
That's a good question, and thank you for mentioning that detail!

The answer is: **No, it doesn't change anything. The same age rule applies regardless of which parent is claiming.**

The eligibility criteria for Child Benefit - including the age limits - are based on the child's circumstances, not on who is making the claim. So whether it's you or Alex's other parent claiming, the rules are exactly the same:

- Child Benefit can only continue for a young person in approved education until 31st August following their 19th birthday
- Since Alex passed that deadline, neither you nor his other parent can claim Child Benefit for him anymore

If Alex's other parent is currently receiving Child Benefit payments for him, those payments should have stopped at the 31st August deadline. If they're still receiving payments, they may want to contact HMRC to ensure the claim is properly closed, as continuing to receive payments they're not entitled to could lead to overpayments that need to be repaid.

I hope that clarifies things! Is there anything else you'd like to know?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, that's very clear. Thank you so much for your help and for explaining everything. I'll let them know about the payments needing to stop if they haven't already. I appreciate your time!

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: The conversation has concluded. The eligibility agent determined that I (playing the role of Alex's parent) am not eligible for Child Benefit because Alex has exceeded the age limit - Child Benefit only continues until August 31st following a young person's 19th birthday when they're in approved education. Since Alex turned 19 in October, that August deadline had already passed before his birthday, making him ineligible despite still being in A-level education.
